title: Selling Transcript Analysis as a $199 Service
description: How tax professionals are packaging IRS transcript analysis as a standalone billable service — and how to price, position, and deliver it.
content:
The $199 Transcript Review Product

A growing number of tax professionals are offering transcript analysis as a standalone engagement — separate from tax prep, separate from representation. The model is simple: a client brings a transcript they don't understand, you run it through the parser, and you deliver a plain-English report with your recommendations.

Why Clients Pay for This

Most taxpayers have no idea how to read an IRS transcript. When they receive a notice or see unusual codes, anxiety drives them to seek professional help. A clear, branded report from a credentialed professional is worth far more than $199 to someone facing IRS uncertainty.

How to Structure the Service

The most effective structure is a flat-fee engagement: one transcript, one report, one 30-minute consultation call. Priced between $150 and $299 depending on your market. No hourly billing, no open-ended scope.

Delivery Workflow

  1. Client submits transcript PDF via your intake form
  2. You run the transcript through the parser (under 10 seconds)
  3. Review the generated report and add your professional notes
  4. Deliver the branded report to the client
  5. Conduct a 30-minute review call
  6. Offer next steps: monitoring membership, representation, or annual review

Upsell Paths

The transcript review is also a powerful entry point. Clients who pay for a one-time review are highly likely to convert to ongoing monitoring memberships or representation engagements when issues are identified.

What to Charge

Survey data from tax professionals using transcript analysis services suggests $149–$299 is the sweet spot for solo practitioners. Firms with multiple preparers can bundle transcript review into onboarding packages at higher price points.
